era_context: Late 14th century: The Ottoman Empire is consolidating power in the Balkans. The Hundred Years' War continues in Western Europe with shifting military fortunes. The Ming Dynasty is firmly established in China under the Hongwu Emperor. The Goryeo Dynasty in Korea is in decline, with military strongmen rising to prominence. This is a period of dynastic consolidation and military competition across Eurasia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica and South America (pre-Columbian sources are sparse)
Central Asia and the Steppe (limited contemporary documentation)

events.title: Treaty of Windsor between England and Portugal
events.description: The Treaty of Windsor formalized an alliance between England and Portugal, establishing a mutual defense pact and commercial relationship. The treaty was negotiated between King John I of Portugal and the English court, and it represented a significant diplomatic achievement for the newly established Aviz Dynasty in Portugal. The alliance would prove durable and would shape European politics for centuries, particularly during the Age of Exploration when English and Portuguese interests often aligned. The treaty also paved the way for the marriage of John I and Philippa of Lancaster the following year.
key_figures: John of Gaunt (Duke of Lancaster, representing English interests)

sources.name: Portuguese chronicles (Fernão Lopes, Crónica de El-Rei D. João I)

events.certainty_note: The Treaty of Windsor is a primary source document and is well-attested in both English and Portuguese records. The date is confirmed as 1386.
cross_references: 1387-001 (Marriage of John I and Philippa of Lancaster)
1385-001 (Battle of Aljubarrota, which enabled John I's consolidation)

events.title: Ottoman expansion in the Balkans continues
events.description: Ottoman forces under Murad I continued their systematic conquest of the Balkans, consolidating control over territories in present-day Serbia, Bulgaria, and Greece. This period saw the Ottoman Empire establishing itself as the dominant military power in the region, displacing Byzantine, Serbian, and Bulgarian authority. The expansion was characterized by a combination of military conquest and political negotiation, with some local rulers accepting Ottoman suzerainty in exchange for maintaining local autonomy. This process would culminate in the Battle of Kosovo in 1389.
events.certainty_note: The general pattern of Ottoman expansion in the Balkans during this period is well-documented, though specific events in 1386 are not individually highlighted in surviving sources. The year represents part of the broader campaign of Murad I.

events.title: Hongwu Emperor's military reorganization
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor continued his systematic reorganization of the Ming military system, establishing the Guard system (Jinyiwei and other imperial guards) and reducing the power of military commanders. These reforms were part of a broader effort to centralize power and prevent military strongmen from challenging imperial authority. The reorganization had significant effects on Ming governance and military effectiveness, establishing patterns that would persist throughout the dynasty.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Official Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The Hongwu Emperor's military reforms are well-documented in official records, though specific events in 1386 are not individually highlighted. The year falls within the broader pattern of consolidation that characterized his entire reign.

events.title: Goryeo Dynasty's continued decline and factional conflict
events.description: The Goryeo Dynasty continued to experience internal instability and factional conflict. Military strongmen, particularly Yi Seong-gye, were rising to prominence and challenging the authority of the Goryeo court. The state was weakened by competing factions, the legacy of Mongol rule, and the rise of new military and administrative elites. By 1386, the dynasty's collapse was imminent, though it would not formally end until 1392. The period was characterized by political instability and the gradual transfer of power from the court to military commanders.
key_figures: Yi Seong-gye (military commander, future founder of Joseon)

sources.name: Goryeosa (History of Goryeo), compiled in Joseon period
Joseon Wangjo Sillok (Annals of the Joseon Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The general instability of late Goryeo is well-documented, though specific events in 1386 are not individually prominent in surviving records. The year falls within the broader pattern of decline that characterized the dynasty's final years.

disconfirming_evidence: The Treaty of Windsor is sometimes presented as a sudden diplomatic breakthrough, but modern scholarship suggests the alliance was the result of longer-term negotiations and mutual interests. The treaty's immediate impact on Portuguese power was limited—Portugal's rise to prominence as a maritime power depended on developments in the 15th century, particularly the rise of Henry the Navigator. Additionally, the Ottoman expansion in the Balkans, while documented in later sources, may have been less rapid and more negotiated than traditional narratives suggest. Some local rulers may have accepted Ottoman suzerainty voluntarily rather than through military conquest.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1386 is moderate for Western Europe and East Asia, but sparse for the Ottoman Empire and the Balkans. English and Portuguese sources are well-preserved and detailed, reflecting the interests of literate elites. Chinese official records are systematic but reflect the Hongwu Emperor's perspective. Ottoman and Byzantine sources are limited to later chronicles, introducing uncertainty about specific dates and events. Korean sources are abundant but were compiled after the Joseon Dynasty's founding, introducing potential bias. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and Central Asia are largely absent from surviving written records for this period.

graph_edges.note: The Treaty of Windsor paved the way for the marriage of John I and Philippa of Lancaster
Ottoman expansion in the Balkans culminated in the Battle of Kosovo
Military reforms continued throughout Hongwu's reign
Goryeo's decline led directly to the founding of the Joseon Dynasty
